The **2002–03 season** was the 123rd season of competitive [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") in England. In this season, Premier League was won by Manchester United, FA Cup was won by Arsenal.

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002%E2%80%9303_in_English_football&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

* [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C.") marked their 25th season of Football League membership by winning the Division Two championship and reaching the league's second tier for the very first time.
* [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C.") was demoted to [Division Two](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division"), just ten years after reaching the finals of both domestic cup competitions and eleven years after coming two places short of the league title.

## Diary of the season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002%E2%80%9303_in_English_football&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Diary of the season")]

* 27 June 2002 – Leeds United sack manager [David O'Leary](/wiki/David_O%27Leary "David O'Leary") after four years in charge. The sacking is thought by many to be down to a combination of both spending more than £100 million on players but never winning a trophy and the publication of his book "Leeds United on Trial", detailing his experiences as manager during the previous season when both Lee Bowyer and Johnathan Woodgate had been on trial for assault.
* 3 July 2002 – [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough_F.C. "Middlesbrough F.C.") pay a club record £8.15million for [Empoli](/wiki/Empoli "Empoli") and [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team") striker [Massimo Maccarone](/wiki/Massimo_Maccarone "Massimo Maccarone").
* 8 July 2002 – Leeds United appoint [Terry Venables](/wiki/Terry_Venables "Terry Venables") as their new manager.
* 10 July 2002 – [AFC Wimbledon](/wiki/AFC_Wimbledon "AFC Wimbledon"), a club founded by former supporters of [Wimbledon](/wiki/Wimbledon_F.C. "Wimbledon F.C.") following The FA's approval of [the latter club's move](/wiki/Relocation_of_Wimbledon_F.C._to_Milton_Keynes "Relocation of Wimbledon F.C. to Milton Keynes") to [Milton Keynes](/wiki/Milton_Keynes "Milton Keynes"), play their first match, losing 4–0 in a preseason friendly to [Sutton United](/wiki/Sutton_United_F.C. "Sutton United F.C.") in front of over 4,600 fans.
* 11 July 2002 – [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") sign defender [Pascal Cygan](/wiki/Pascal_Cygan "Pascal Cygan") from [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille") for £2.1million.
* 12 July 2002 – Newcastle United sign 20-year-old defender [Titus Bramble](/wiki/Titus_Bramble "Titus Bramble") from [Ipswich Town](/wiki/Ipswich_Town_F.C. "Ipswich Town F.C.") for £5million.
* 22 July 2002 – [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") break the English transfer record for the third time in just over a year. They pay [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_A.F.C. "Leeds United A.F.C.") £29 million for central defender [Rio Ferdinand](/wiki/Rio_Ferdinand "Rio Ferdinand"), amidst reports that the Yorkshire club are in severe financial difficulties after overspending during the previous 3 seasons, and having a crippling loan debt.
* 24 July 2002 – [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") sign Arsenal goalkeeper [Richard Wright](/wiki/Richard_Wright_(footballer) "Richard Wright (footballer)") for £3.5million.
* 26 July 2002 – Manchester United sell out-of-favour striker [Dwight Yorke](/wiki/Dwight_Yorke "Dwight Yorke") to [Blackburn Rovers](/wiki/Blackburn_Rovers_F.C. "Blackburn Rovers F.C.") for £2million, with [Graeme Souness](/wiki/Graeme_Souness "Graeme Souness") partnering Yorke with his former Manchester United strike partner [Andy Cole](/wiki/Andy_Cole "Andy Cole").
* 30 July 2002 – [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C."), newly promoted to the Premier League, pay a club record £4.25million for [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.") striker [Clinton Morrison](/wiki/Clinton_Morrison "Clinton Morrison").
* 2 August 2002 – [Juninho](/wiki/Juninho_Paulista "Juninho Paulista") signs for Middlesbrough for the third time in a £6million move from [Atlético Madrid](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Madrid "Atlético Madrid").
* 4 August 2002 – [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") move into their new 32,500-seat [Walkers Stadium](/wiki/Walkers_Stadium "Walkers Stadium") after 111 years at [Filbert Street](/wiki/Filbert_Street "Filbert Street"). They drew 1–1 in a friendly with [Athletic Bilbao](/wiki/Athletic_Bilbao "Athletic Bilbao").
* 5 August 2002 – Middlesbrough sign [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") midfielder [George Boateng](/wiki/George_Boateng "George Boateng") for £5million.
* 7 August 2002 – [Players' Union](/wiki/Players%27_Union "Players' Union") chairman [Gordon Taylor](/wiki/Gordon_Taylor_(footballer) "Gordon Taylor (footballer)") calls for the [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League"), currently in a financial crisis following the collapse of [ITV Digital](/wiki/ITV_Digital "ITV Digital"), to combine with the 12 [Scottish Premier League](/wiki/Scottish_Premier_League "Scottish Premier League") clubs to form an expanded Football League. There has recently been talk of [Rangers](/wiki/Rangers_F.C. "Rangers F.C.") and [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_F.C. "Celtic F.C.") resigning from the Scottish Premier League to join [Division One](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") of the English league, and for several years it has frequently been suggested by various sources that the two clubs should join the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League").[[1]](#cite_note-1)
* 8 August 2002 – Leeds United sign Liverpool midfielder [Nick Barmby](/wiki/Nick_Barmby "Nick Barmby") for £2.75million.
* 15 August 2002 – Liverpool sell defender [Stephen Wright](/wiki/Stephen_Wright_(English_footballer) "Stephen Wright (English footballer)") to [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") for £3million.
* 17 August 2002 – The [FA Premier League](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") season begins. [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.")'s first top division game for 17 years ends in a 1–0 away defeat to [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.").
* 21 August 2002 – Sunderland sign [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") midfielder [Matt Piper](/wiki/Matt_Piper "Matt Piper") for £3.5million.
* 29 August 2002 – [Lee Hughes](/wiki/Lee_Hughes "Lee Hughes") rejoins [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") for £2.5million after a year at [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C."), and is joined for £2.25million by [Tranmere Rovers](/wiki/Tranmere_Rovers "Tranmere Rovers") midfielder [Jason Koumas](/wiki/Jason_Koumas "Jason Koumas").
* 30 August 2002 – Sunderland sign striker [Marcus Stewart](/wiki/Marcus_Stewart "Marcus Stewart") from Ipswich Town for £3.25million.
* 31 August 2002
  + [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C.") sell striker [Robbie Keane](/wiki/Robbie_Keane "Robbie Keane") to [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C.") for £7 million, as [Glenn Hoddle](/wiki/Glenn_Hoddle "Glenn Hoddle")'s team look to push for the Premier League title after three wins and a draw from their opening four games take them to the top of the league as August draws to a close. Arsenal (defending champions), Liverpool, Leeds United and Charlton Athletic complete the top five. Bolton Wanderers, who have lost both of their games this month, prop up the top flight, while winless West Ham United and Southampton complete the bottom three. Sunderland pay a club record £6.75million for [Rangers](/wiki/Rangers_F.C. "Rangers F.C.") striker [Tore Andre Flo](/wiki/Tore_Andre_Flo "Tore Andre Flo").[[2]](#cite_note-2) West Bromwich Albion beat Fulham 1–0 to record their first top division win since March 1986.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
  + [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C."), who were generally not considered as serious promotion challenge, are the surprise leaders of Division One, with their nearest competition coming from last season's beaten playoff finalists [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C."). [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C."), [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C."), [Rotherham United](/wiki/Rotherham_United_F.C. "Rotherham United F.C.") and [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C.") complete the top six.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
* 10 September 2002
  + Sunderland sign goalkeeper [Mart Poom](/wiki/Mart_Poom "Mart Poom") from [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.") for £2.5million.
  + A [Kevin Nolan](/wiki/Kevin_Nolan "Kevin Nolan") goal gives Bolton Wanderers a surprise 1–0 league win over Manchester United at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford").
* 14 September 2002 – Manchester United's dismal start to the season continues as they lose 1–0 at Leeds United. They are now ninth in the league, one place below a newly promoted West Bromwich Albion side who have achieved three consecutive league wins.
* 16 September 2002 – Birmingham City beat Aston Villa 3–0 at St Andrew's in the first [Second City derby](/wiki/Second_City_derby "Second City derby") in the league for 15 years, and the first in the top flight for 17 years.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
* 17 September 2002 – After a slow start to the season, the pressure is growing on Sunderland manager Peter Reid. The [Daily Mirror](/wiki/Daily_Mirror "Daily Mirror") tips [George Graham](/wiki/George_Graham_(footballer,_born_1944) "George Graham (footballer, born 1944)") to be named as Sunderland manager if Reid is ousted.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
* 21 September 2002 – [Ruud van Nistelrooy](/wiki/Ruud_van_Nistelrooy "Ruud van Nistelrooy") scores the winning goal from the penalty spot as Manchester United get back on track with a 1–0 home league win over Tottenham.
* 23 September 2002 – Peter Reid gains unexpected support from Sir Bobby Robson, manager of Sunderland's local rivals Newcastle United, who defends him over the club's recent shortcomings.[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* 26 September 2002 – A poll among Sunderland supporters names David O'Leary as the man they would most like to see as manager in place of under-fire Peter Reid.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
* 30 September 2002 – September finishes with defending champions Arsenal top of the Premier League, two points ahead of second-placed Liverpool. Manchester United, held back by two defeats this month, are fourth in the league and level on points with third-placed Middlesbrough who are mounting a surprise title challenge. Chelsea occupy fifth place, with last month's leaders Tottenham Hotspur now down to sixth. West Ham United, Charlton Athletic and Southampton occupy the bottom three places.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Portsmouth and Leicester City lead the way in Division One, with the playoff zone being occupied by Norwich City, [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C."), Rotherham United and [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C.").[[10]](#cite_note-portsmouth-mad1-10)
* 7 October 2002 – [Peter Reid](/wiki/Peter_Reid "Peter Reid") is sacked by [FA Premier League](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") strugglers [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") after seven-and-a-half years in charge.
* 9 October 2002 – Republic of Ireland manager [Mick McCarthy](/wiki/Mick_McCarthy "Mick McCarthy") is named as a contender for the Sunderland manager's job.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* 10 October 2002 – After just four months as manager of [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C."), manager [Steve Cotterill](/wiki/Steve_Cotterill "Steve Cotterill") leaves the club with a statement revealing that he has agreed to take a role at another club, sparking speculation that he has been appointed as Sunderland's new manager. Hours later, however, it was revealed that [Howard Wilkinson](/wiki/Howard_Wilkinson "Howard Wilkinson") has ended his five-year role as technical director of [The Football Association](/wiki/The_Football_Association "The Football Association") to become Sunderland's new manager, with Cotterill as his assistant.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* 12 October 2002 – [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") open their [Euro 2004](/wiki/Euro_2004 "Euro 2004") qualifying series with a 2–1 win over [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_national_football_team "Slovakia national football team") in [Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava"). [David Beckham](/wiki/David_Beckham "David Beckham") and [Michael Owen](/wiki/Michael_Owen "Michael Owen") score for England.
* 19 October 2002 – [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") striker [Wayne Rooney](/wiki/Wayne_Rooney "Wayne Rooney") becomes the youngest-ever goalscorer in [FA Premier League](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") history when he scores a last-minute winner against [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C."), five days before his 17th birthday, to end the opposition's 30-match unbeaten Premiership run. West Ham United winger [Trevor Sinclair](/wiki/Trevor_Sinclair "Trevor Sinclair") takes the pressure off his manager [Glenn Roeder](/wiki/Glenn_Roeder "Glenn Roeder") by scoring the only goal in an away league win over Sunderland, who are playing their first game under the management of Howard Wilkinson.
* 26 October 2002 – After waiting 12 years to renew old rivalries, [Andrew Johnson](/wiki/Andrew_Johnson_(English_footballer) "Andrew Johnson (English footballer)") scores a hat-trick as Crystal Palace crush Brighton 5–0 in a Division One game at Selhurst Park.
* 27 October 2002 – [James Beattie](/wiki/James_Beattie_(footballer) "James Beattie (footballer)") scores a hat-trick as Southampton beat Fulham 4–2 in the league at [St Mary's Stadium](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Stadium "St Mary's Stadium").
* 30 October 2002 – [Adam Crozier](/wiki/Adam_Crozier "Adam Crozier") announced his resignation as chief executive of [The Football Association](/wiki/The_Football_Association "The Football Association") after two years in this role.[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* 31 October 2002 – October draws to a close with Liverpool having leapfrogged Arsenal into second place, opening up a four-point gap and looking in a stronger position than ever before to end their league title wait that began in 1990. Chelsea, Manchester United and Tottenham Hotspur complete the top five. Bolton Wanderers, Sunderland and West Bromwich Albion occupy the three relegation places.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Portsmouth now have a seven-point lead over nearest rivals Leicester City at the top of Division One. The playoff zone is occupied by Norwich City, Sheffield United, [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C.") and Nottingham Forest.[[10]](#cite_note-portsmouth-mad1-10)
* 9 November 2002 – A [Shaun Goater](/wiki/Shaun_Goater "Shaun Goater") double sees [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") beat [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") 3–1 in the last Manchester derby at [Maine Road](/wiki/Maine_Road "Maine Road"). It is the first time since September 1989 that City have beaten United in a competitive game, and the first time since March 1993 that United have failed to beat City at Maine Road.
* 18 December 2002 – Football mourns the deaths of [Football Association](/wiki/The_Football_Association "The Football Association") chairman [Sir Bert Millichip](/wiki/Bert_Millichip "Bert Millichip"), 88, and former [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") and [Shrewsbury Town](/wiki/Shrewsbury_Town_F.C. "Shrewsbury Town F.C.") striker Arthur Rowley, 76, who scored a record 434 league goals during his 19-year career.
* 10 November 2002 – Sunderland achieve their first league win under Howard Wilkinson by beating Tottenham 2–0 at the [Stadium of Light](/wiki/Stadium_of_Light "Stadium of Light").
* 23 November 2002 – Ruud van Nistelrooy scores a hat-trick as Manchester United end a dismal run of league form by beating Newcastle United 5–3 at Old Trafford.
* 30 November 2002 – As November draws to a close, Liverpool are on a dismal run of form which has seen Arsenal overtake them into first place in the Premier League by a single point. Everton are putting up a surprise title challenge after more than a decade of mediocrity as they occupy third place and are just three points off the top. Chelsea and Manchester United complete the top five. West Ham United, West Bromwich Albion and Bolton Wanderers finish the month in the relegation zone.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Portsmouth and Leicester City continue to lead the way in Division One. Nottingham Forest, Norwich City, [Reading](/wiki/Reading_F.C. "Reading F.C.") (newly promoted) and Sheffield United complete the top six.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* 7 December 2002 – Manchester United's revival continues with a 2–0 home league win over defending champions Arsenal.
* 14 December 2002 – Manchester United continue to close in on the top of the table with a 3–0 home win over West Ham. Sunderland boost their survival challenge with a 2–1 home win over Liverpool, whose title challenge is continuing to fall away. [Marc-Vivien Foé](/wiki/Marc-Vivien_Fo%C3%A9 "Marc-Vivien Foé") scores twice for Manchester City in a 2–2 draw at Charlton Athletic.
* 26 December 2002 – [Hull City](/wiki/Hull_City_A.F.C. "Hull City A.F.C.") celebrate their first competitive game at their new [Kingston Communications Stadium](/wiki/KC_Stadium "KC Stadium") with a 2–0 win over [Hartlepool United](/wiki/Hartlepool_United_F.C. "Hartlepool United F.C.") in [Division Three](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division").
* 4 January 2003 – [Shrewsbury Town](/wiki/Shrewsbury_Town "Shrewsbury Town") beat Everton 2–1 in an FA Cup third round tie at [Gay Meadow](/wiki/Gay_Meadow "Gay Meadow").
* 31 December 2002 – 2002 ends with Arsenal top of the Premier League, four points ahead of second-placed Chelsea and with a game in hand. Manchester United occupy third place, Everton are fourth and Liverpool's ongoing decline has seen them slip to fifth place just two months after they had a comfortable lead at the top. Southampton's recent resurgence is putting pressure on the top five as they occupy seventh place and look on course for their highest finish since 1995. West Ham United remain bottom and are still looking for their first home win of the season, while West Bromwich Albion and Sunderland complete the bottom three.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Portsmouth and Leicester City's two-horse race at the top of Division One continues, with the playoff zone unchanged from last month.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
* 12 January 2003 – [Robbie Keane](/wiki/Robbie_Keane "Robbie Keane") scores a hat-trick as Tottenham beat Everton 4–3 in the league at [White Hart Lane](/wiki/White_Hart_Lane "White Hart Lane").
* 19 January 2003 – [Thierry Henry](/wiki/Thierry_Henry "Thierry Henry") scores a hat-trick as Arsenal beat West Ham United 3–1 at [Highbury](/wiki/Arsenal_Stadium "Arsenal Stadium").
* 30 January 2003 – Still struggling with debt, Leeds United sell [Robbie Fowler](/wiki/Robbie_Fowler "Robbie Fowler") to Manchester City for £6million.
* 31 January 2003 – January draws to a close with Arsenal still top of the Premier League, two points above second placed Manchester United and within a game in hand. Newcastle United, Chelsea and Everton complete the top five. Liverpool's decline continues as they occupy eighth place, deepening fears that a side looking all set for title glory three months ago might now not even qualify for the UEFA Cup. Meanwhile, West Bromwich Albion's dismal form continues as they and West Ham United prop up the rest of the top flight on goal difference. Sunderland occupy the last relegation place. Middlesbrough boost their bid for a [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") place by spending a total of £8.5million on new signings [Chris Riggott](/wiki/Chris_Riggott "Chris Riggott"), [Malcolm Christie](/wiki/Malcolm_Christie "Malcolm Christie") and [Michael Ricketts](/wiki/Michael_Ricketts "Michael Ricketts"). The true extent of Leeds United's financial difficulties becomes public knowledge during a press conference to announce the sale of [Jonathan Woodgate](/wiki/Jonathan_Woodgate "Jonathan Woodgate") to Newcastle United for £9million, during which Terry Venables states that he had been assured players wouldn't need to be sold.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Portsmouth and Leicester City remain in pole position at the top of Division One, and apart from Coventry City edging Reading out of the playoff zone the top six of the division is unchanged from last month.[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* 9 February 2003 – Manchester United drop two priceless points in the Premier League title race as they can only manage a 1–1 draw with City in the [Manchester derby](/wiki/Manchester_derby "Manchester derby") at Old Trafford. It is the first time since the 1991–92 season that they have failed to beat City in either derby games.
* 12 February 2003 – A record of eight black players are capped in the England side's 3–1 home defeated by [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team") at Upton Park.[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* 16 February 2003 – One of the most decisive games of the season sees Arsenal cancel out Manchester United in [the double](/wiki/Double_(association_football) "Double (association football)") race with a 2–0 win at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford"). After the game, [David Beckham](/wiki/David_Beckham "David Beckham") suffers a gash to his face which is reportedly the result of Sir Alex Ferguson throwing a basket full of boots across the changing room after his side's defeat.
* 22 February 2003 – Manchester United drop points once again when relegation threatened Bolton hold them to a 1–1 draw at the [Reebok Stadium](/wiki/Reebok_Stadium "Reebok Stadium").
* 28 February 2003 – The month-end leaders for February are Arsenal, who now lead Manchester United by five points. Newcastle United, Chelsea and Everton complete the top five. There will now be a place in the UEFA Cup for the Premier League's sixth place team if Manchester United or Liverpool (who contest this weekend's League Cup final) finish in the top five. Currently in sixth place are Charlton Athletic, while Liverpool are still only seventh in a division which they had looked set to win a few months ago.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Portsmouth have a two-point lead over Leicester City at the top of Division One. Reading, Nottingham Forest and Sheffield United remain in the playoff zone, but an improving Wolverhampton Wanderers side have edged Norwich City out of the top six. Rotherham United are in contention for promotion after an impressive season so far.[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* 2 March 2003 – [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") win the League Cup for the seventh time in their history thanks to a 2–0 win over [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") in the final at [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") in [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff"). On the same day, Arsenal establish an eight-point lead at the top of the league and look all set for a second successive league title.
* 5 March 2003 – Manchester United cut Arsenal's Premier League lead to five points with a 2–1 home win over struggling Leeds United.
* 10 March 2003 – Howard Wilkinson is sacked as [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") manager (along with his assistant Steve Cotterill) after his team won just two out of 20 Premiership matches under his management. The hunt for his successor begins, with Mick McCarthy, David O'Leary and George Burley the three names being particularly strongly linked with the vacancy.[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* 12 March 2003 – Sunderland appoint former [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland_national_football_team "Republic of Ireland national football team") national coach [Mick McCarthy](/wiki/Mick_McCarthy "Mick McCarthy") as their new manager.
* 15 March 2003 – Arsenal are now just two points ahead of Manchester United at the top of the league, after they lose 2–0 at Blackburn and their rivals win 1–0 at Aston Villa.
* 19 March 2003 – Two goals in three minutes for Forest sets them on their way to a 3–0 victory over Derby County.[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* 21 March 2003 – [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C."), 16th in the [FA Premier League](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") and £120million in debt, sack [Terry Venables](/wiki/Terry_Venables "Terry Venables") after eight months in charge. Peter Reid is named as his successor on a temporary contract until the end of the season, but it is far from clear whether Reid will get the job on a permanent basis.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
* 22 March 2003
  + [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.") (in [Division One](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division")) suspend manager [John Gregory](/wiki/John_Gregory_(footballer) "John Gregory (footballer)") over allegations of misconduct and replace him with [George Burley](/wiki/George_Burley "George Burley") as interim manager.
  + Manchester United beat Fulham 3–0 at Old Trafford, with Ruud van Nistelrooy scoring all three goals, to go top of the Premier League just three weeks after their title hopes had appeared dead.
* 23 March 2003 – Arsenal return to the top of the Premier League with a narrow 2–1 win over Everton.
* 31 March 2003 – March draws to a close with Manchester United now leading the Premier League just a month after Arsenal looked to have won it, though [Arsène Wenger](/wiki/Ars%C3%A8ne_Wenger "Arsène Wenger")'s team are only a point behind with a game in hand.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Three points now separate Portsmouth and Leicester City at the top of Division One. Sheffield United, Reading, Nottingham Forest and Wolverhampton Wanderers remain in the playoff zone. Ipswich Town's resurgence has seen them creep to the fringe of the top six, but Rotherham United's playoff hopes are evaporating.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* 5 April 2003 – Manchester United seize the initiative in the title challenge by beating Liverpool 4–0 at Old Trafford as Arsenal can only manage a 1–1 draw at Aston Villa. Leeds United move closer to survival with a 6–1 away win over a Charlton side which effectively ends their opposition's hopes of European qualification.
* 12 April 2003 – [Paul Scholes](/wiki/Paul_Scholes "Paul Scholes") scores a hat-trick as Manchester United beat Newcastle United 6–2 on [Tyneside](/wiki/Tyneside "Tyneside") to extend their lead at the top of the Premier League. Newcastle's neighbours Sunderland's relegation is confirmed by a 2–0 defeat at Birmingham City.
* 16 April 2003 – The much-anticipated "title decider" between Arsenal and Manchester United at [Highbury](/wiki/Arsenal_Stadium "Arsenal Stadium") ends in a 2–2 draw, meaning that Arsenal are now three points behind Manchester United but with a game in hand and a remotely superior goal difference.[[29]](#cite_note-manchesterunited-mad1-29)
* 19 April 2003 – Paul Scholes is on the scoresheet twice as a 3–1 home win over Blackburn Rovers edges Manchester United closer to their eighth Premier League title in 11 seasons, despite Arsenal's 2–0 win at Middlesbrough. West Bromwich Albion are relegated despite a 2–1 away win over already doomed Sunderland. [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") seal promotion to the Premier League with a 2–0 win over Brighton.
* 21 April 2003 – West Ham manager Glenn Roeder is hospitalised with a suspected stroke; director of football [Trevor Brooking](/wiki/Trevor_Brooking "Trevor Brooking") is placed in charge of the first team until Roeder is well enough to resume control.[[30]](#cite_note-30)
* 26 April 2003 – [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") suffer a huge blow to their double hopes, as they surrender a 2–0 lead in their game-in-hand to draw 2–2 with [Bolton](/wiki/Bolton_Wanderers_F.C. "Bolton Wanderers F.C.") at the [Reebok Stadium](/wiki/Reebok_Stadium "Reebok Stadium") – well and truly shifting the title race in [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.")'s favour with three games to go.
* 27 April 2003 – [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C.") beat [Rotherham](/wiki/Rotherham_United_F.C. "Rotherham United F.C.") 3–2 to win the Division One championship and gain promotion to the Premier League for the first time since the league's inception. Apart from the [1987–88 season](/wiki/1987%E2%80%9388_in_English_football "1987–88 in English football"), they have not played top division football since the 1950s. On the same day, [Alan Shearer](/wiki/Alan_Shearer "Alan Shearer") receives the Premier League "Player of the Decade" award at a [PFA](/wiki/Professional_Footballers%27_Association "Professional Footballers' Association") ceremony.[[31]](#cite_note-31)
* 29 April 2003 – Glenn Roeder's illness is diagnosed as a brain tumour which will be operated upon shortly.[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* 30 April 2003 – Sunderland, without a point since January and win since December, end the month, and West Bromwich Albion, with only six wins in their first top division campaign for nearly 20 years, end April with their relegation battle lost. West Ham United complete the bottom three but their survival bid is still very much alive as they are posing a threat to Bolton Wanderers, Birmingham City, Fulham, Aston Villa and a Leeds United team who began the season among the teams expected to challenge for the title but are now battling relegation as well as huge debts.[[29]](#cite_note-manchesterunited-mad1-29)
* 3 May 2003 – A Ruud van Nistelrooy hat-trick contributes to Manchester United's 4–1 home win over Charlton Athletic and puts them on the brink of title glory.
* 4 May 2003 – [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") lose 3–2 at home to [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C."), a result which ensures Leeds United's safety and ends Arsenal's defence of the title. [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") are crowned league champions for the eighth time in 11 seasons. [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") are relegated from the [Premiership](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") with a record low of 4 wins, 19 points and 21 goals. [Shrewsbury Town](/wiki/Shrewsbury_Town_F.C. "Shrewsbury Town F.C.") are relegated to the [Conference](/wiki/Football_Conference "Football Conference"), ending 53 years of Football League membership. [Exeter City](/wiki/Exeter_City_F.C. "Exeter City F.C.") finished 23rd in Division Three and are also relegated to the [Conference](/wiki/Football_Conference "Football Conference") – the first club to suffer automatic relegation without finishing bottom of the league. [Peter Schmeichel](/wiki/Peter_Schmeichel "Peter Schmeichel") calls time on his footballing career six months before his 40th birthday after helping [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") to finish ninth in the Premiership.
* 9 May 2003 – Derby County confirm that suspended manager John Gregory will not be returning, and his contract is terminated to make way for George Burley to receive the job on a permanent basis.
* 11 May 2003 – West Ham United are relegated after 10 years in the Premier League after failing to get the better of a Birmingham side who hold them to a 2–2 draw. Bolton are safe after a 2–1 home win over Middlesbrough. Sunderland's 4–0 home defeat to Arsenal sees them end the season as officially the worst Premier League team ever with a record low of four wins, 19 points and 21 goals. Not since [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C.") in the old First Division 18 years ago has any top flight club attained such a poor record in the top flight.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Ninth placed Manchester City bid farewell to [Maine Road](/wiki/Maine_Road "Maine Road") after 80 years, their last game before relocation to the [City of Manchester Stadium](/wiki/City_of_Manchester_Stadium "City of Manchester Stadium") being watched by a 35,000 capacity crowd as they lost 1–0 to eighth placed Southampton. The final goal at the stadium is scored by Saints defender [Michael Svensson](/wiki/Michael_Svensson "Michael Svensson").[[34]](#cite_note-34)
* 17 May 2003 – [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") ease their Premier League disappointment by winning the FA Cup for the ninth time in their history, beating [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton_F.C. "Southampton F.C.") 1–0 at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") courtesy of a first-half [Robert Pires](/wiki/Robert_Pires "Robert Pires") goal.
* 22 May 2003 – [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") sign [Joseph Yobo](/wiki/Joseph_Yobo "Joseph Yobo") from [Olympique Marseille](/wiki/Olympique_Marseille "Olympique Marseille") for £3.5million.
* 26 May 2003 – [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") return to the top flight after a 19-year exile by beating [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C.") 3–0 in the [Division One](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") playoff final.
* 4 June 2003 – After 13 years as goalkeeper for Arsenal, [David Seaman](/wiki/David_Seaman "David Seaman") joins Manchester City on a free transfer, signing a one-year contract.[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* 17 June 2003 – [David Beckham](/wiki/David_Beckham "David Beckham") agrees to join [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid "Real Madrid") in a £25 million deal after 12 years at Manchester United.
* 26 June 2003 – [Marc-Vivien Foé](/wiki/Marc-Vivien_Fo%C3%A9 "Marc-Vivien Foé"), who spent the 2002–03 season on loan to [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C."), collapses and dies at the age of 28 during a [Confederations Cup](/wiki/Confederations_Cup "Confederations Cup") tie for [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon_national_football_team "Cameroon national football team").

### FA Premier League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002%E2%80%9303_in_English_football&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: FA Premier League")]

Main article: [2002–03 FA Premier League](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_FA_Premier_League "2002–03 FA Premier League")

Manchester United, unbeaten in the league after 26 December, overhauled Arsenal during the final three months of the season to clinch their eighth Premiership title in 11 seasons, while the other two Champions League places went to Newcastle United and Chelsea. Going into the UEFA Cup were Liverpool (who made an excellent start to the league, going unbeaten in their first three months before a sudden collapse and falling to 7th but with a late good form to finish 5th) and Blackburn Rovers who were bouncing in mid table at February, although they missed out on Champions League qualification thanks to staying in mid table for a long time, finished 6th, along with FA Cup runners-up Southampton (who also achieved their best Premiership finish of 8th place) and Fair Play award winners Manchester City (who finished an impressive ninth on their Premiership comeback). Southampton had not played in European competitions for nearly 20 years, while Manchester City's last European campaign was a quarter of a century ago.

Everton finished seventh in their first full season under the management of [David Moyes](/wiki/David_Moyes "David Moyes"), the club's highest finish for seven years, in a campaign where the club was the centre of national media and public attention following the performances of 17-year-old striker [Wayne Rooney](/wiki/Wayne_Rooney "Wayne Rooney"), who became the youngest full England international after the season's end.

Compensation for Arsenal after their spectacular failure to retain the league title came in the form of retaining the FA Cup – the first side to do so for [21 years](/wiki/1982_FA_Cup_Final "1982 FA Cup Final").

Leeds United's season was plagued by a loss of form and mounting debts, and having to sell several key players, as they slipped to 15th place – their lowest finish for 10 years and a stark contrast to the previous five campaigns, where they had never finished outside the top five. Manager [Terry Venables](/wiki/Terry_Venables "Terry Venables") was axed towards the end of March after eight months in charge, with [Peter Reid](/wiki/Peter_Reid "Peter Reid") taking over for the final weeks of the season to secure survival and earn himself a permanent contract. Aston Villa's 16th-place finish was their lowest for eight years, although their strong home form ensured their survival in the Premier League. However, they sat above Blackburn Rovers in February but only poor form at the end prevented them from qualifying for the UEFA Cup.

Sunderland's season started badly and got worse as they finished with a record Premiership low of 4 wins, 19 points and 21 goals, losing their final 15 league games in the process. The [Wearside](/wiki/Wearside "Wearside") club went through three managers, with Peter Reid (one of the league's longest-serving managers) leaving in early October to be replaced by [Howard Wilkinson](/wiki/Howard_Wilkinson "Howard Wilkinson"), who lasted just five months before [Mick McCarthy](/wiki/Mick_McCarthy "Mick McCarthy") was appointed. They were joined by West Bromwich Albion, who attained just 26 points in their first top flight season for nearly 20 years and won just three times in the league after September. Last to go down were West Ham United, who went down with 42 points – the highest points tally of any club to be relegated from the Premiership under the 20-club format.

### [Football League First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002%E2%80%9303_in_English_football&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Football League First Division")]

Main article: [2002–03 Football League First Division](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_Football_League_First_Division "2002–03 Football League First Division")

Portsmouth won the Division One title by some distance, passing manager [Harry Redknapp](/wiki/Harry_Redknapp "Harry Redknapp")'s old club, West Ham, on the way down and ending their own 15-year exile from the top flight. They were rarely outside the top two at any point of the season.

Leicester City earned a somewhat controversial promotion, as administration and a Creditor's Voluntary Agreement wrote off much of their £30million debt. Partly as a result of this, the League would introduce a 10-point subtraction for any teams entering administration from the next season onwards. The play-offs were won by Wolves, returning to the top flight after nearly 20 years and finally allowing owner [Sir Jack Hayward](/wiki/Sir_Jack_Hayward "Sir Jack Hayward") to see the return he wanted on his years of investment. Their opponents, Sheffield United, were semi-finalists in both domestic cups.

Gillingham enjoyed their most successful season to date, finishing a club record eleventh place in the division, the highest in their history. Despite having some of the lowest attendances in senior football after the relocation to [Milton Keynes](/wiki/Milton_Keynes "Milton Keynes") was announced, Wimbledon managed a 10th-place finish in the league, but then went into administration – putting the future of the club under yet more doubt.

Grimsby were relegated, after struggling in the division for five years on extremely limited resources. Both Brighton and Sheffield Wednesday suffered awful starts to the season (Brighton managing only a single win from their first sixteen matches), and despite good runs of form late in the season, neither were able to survive, although Brighton at least had the satisfaction of staying in the hunt for survival up to the last game.

### [Football League Second Division](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002%E2%80%9303_in_English_football&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Football League Second Division")]

Main article: [2002–03 Football League § Second Division](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_Football_League#Second_Division "2002–03 Football League")

Wigan won their first-ever promotion beyond the Second Division. Crewe managed a promotion on the back of their famed youth policy while play-off winners Cardiff were another big-spending team that were able to earn promotion, beating QPR in the final.

Tranmere Rovers had to settle for seventh place – not even enough for a playoff place – despite collecting 80 points, a tally which at times had been enough for some teams to win automatic promotion. Plymouth Argyle and Luton Town both achieved top half finishes a season after promotion.

An ill-advised managerial change mid-season helped send Northampton down. Mansfield Town's first season out of the bottom division in over a decade ended as their previous spell had; with immediate relegation in a season awash with over 160 goals where they had no problems scoring goals but unfortunately leaked goals at an alarming rate. Huddersfield started badly, and a financial crisis later in the season helped condemn them to relegation, only three years after they looked Premiership-bound. Cheltenham came close to survival, but a defeat on the final day of the season saw them return to Division Three.

### [Football League Third Division](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002%E2%80%9303_in_English_football&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Football League Third Division")]

Main article: [2002–03 Football League § Third Division](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_Football_League#Third_Division "2002–03 Football League")

Rushden & Diamonds continued their meteoric rise, winning the divisional title. They were helped in no small part by runners-up Hartlepool suffering a shocking late-season collapse, which cost them the title and manager [Mike Newell](/wiki/Mike_Newell_(footballer) "Mike Newell (footballer)") his job. Wrexham took the last automatic promotion spot and bounced back from the previous season's relegation, as did play-off winners [AFC Bournemouth](/wiki/AFC_Bournemouth "AFC Bournemouth"), who beat Lincoln City in the final.

Oxford United were promotion contenders a season after finishing fourth from bottom, but in the end just missed out on the playoffs. Despite almost going out of business, York City managed to finish 10th – their highest finish since relegation to Division Three in 1999. Hull City finished 13th under new manager [Peter Taylor](/wiki/Peter_Taylor_(footballer_born_1953) "Peter Taylor (footballer born 1953)"), who took over from [Jan Molby](/wiki/Jan_Molby "Jan Molby") just before the club completed its relocation from [Boothferry Park](/wiki/Boothferry_Park "Boothferry Park") to the new [KC Stadium](/wiki/Kingston_Communications_Stadium "Kingston Communications Stadium"), which gave the club the highest crowds at this level, although the upturn in form was not enough to get them anywhere near the top of the table. Boston United managed a 15th-place finish in their first season as a league club, despite having four points deducted for financial irregularities.

A shock FA Cup victory over Everton did little to help Shrewsbury, and they finished bottom of the League. Exeter City were bought out pre-season in a high-profile takeover spearheaded by [Uri Geller](/wiki/Uri_Geller "Uri Geller"); unfortunately, Geller's associates proceeded to asset-strip the club, and despite a late-season run of form, Exeter fell victim to the first-ever dual relegation from the League.

[Yeovil Town](/wiki/Yeovil_Town_F.C. "Yeovil Town F.C."), who had spent decades making the headlines by defeating league opposition in the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup"), finally reached the Football League as Conference champions. The first Conference playoffs were won by [Doncaster Rovers](/wiki/Doncaster_Rovers_F.C. "Doncaster Rovers F.C."), who returned to the league after a five-year exile with the financial backing of [John Ryan](/wiki/John_Ryan_(businessman) "John Ryan (businessman)"), who now had a new [all-seater stadium](/wiki/Keepmoat_Stadium "Keepmoat Stadium") in the pipeline.

* [Jonathan Woodgate](/wiki/Jonathan_Woodgate "Jonathan Woodgate") from [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C.") to [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C."), £9m

For subsequent transfer deals see [2003–04 in English football](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_in_English_football "2003–04 in English football").

## Deaths

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002%E2%80%9303_in_English_football&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Deaths")]

* 7 July 2002: [Ray Wood](/wiki/Ray_Wood "Ray Wood"), 71, [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") and [England](/wiki/English_national_football_team "English national football team") goalkeeper, who was a survivor of the Munich Air Disaster and won two league championship medals.
* 17 August 2002: [John Charles](/wiki/John_Charles_(footballer_born_1944) "John Charles (footballer born 1944)"), 57, played 118 league games at full-back for his only club [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.") between 1962 and 1971 before his playing career was ended by injury. He died of cancer, which had been diagnosed in 2001.
* 5 November 2002: [Rob Hindmarch](/wiki/Rob_Hindmarch "Rob Hindmarch"), 41, former [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C."), [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers "Wolverhampton Wanderers") and [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.") centre-half, died of motor neurone disease.
* 30 November 2002: [Alan Ashman](/wiki/Alan_Ashman "Alan Ashman"), 74, former football manager who took [Carlisle United](/wiki/Carlisle_United_F.C. "Carlisle United F.C.") into the old First Division in 1974 and [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") to F.A Cup glory in 1968.
* 18 December 2002:
  + – [Bert Millichip](/wiki/Bert_Millichip "Bert Millichip"), 88, chairman of [The Football Association](/wiki/The_Football_Association "The Football Association") from 1981 until 1996 and a former director of West Bromwich Albion.
  + – [Angus Morrison](/wiki/Angus_Morrison_(footballer) "Angus Morrison (footballer)"), 78, played 329 games in the English league after moving from his native [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") in 1946, scoring a total of 95 goals from the left wing for [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C."), [Preston North End](/wiki/Preston_North_End_F.C. "Preston North End F.C.") and [Millwall](/wiki/Millwall_F.C. "Millwall F.C."). Although he did not collect any silverware, he was on the losing side with Preston in the 1954 FA Cup Final.
* 19 December 2002: [Arthur Rowley](/wiki/Arthur_Rowley "Arthur Rowley"), 76, a former centre forward of [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") player and [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") record scorer who scored an English league career record of 434 goals.
* 31 December 2002: [Billy Morris](/wiki/Billy_Morris_(footballer,_born_1918) "Billy Morris (footballer, born 1918)"), 84, was an FA Cup runner-up with [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C.") in 1947, and scored 47 league goals for the [Turf Moor](/wiki/Turf_Moor "Turf Moor") club. He was capped five times by [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team") and had two short spells as [Wrexham](/wiki/Wrexham_F.C. "Wrexham F.C.") manager in the 1960s.
* 3 February 2003: [Trevor Morris](/wiki/Trevor_Morris_(footballer) "Trevor Morris (footballer)"), 82, former [Ipswich Town](/wiki/Ipswich_Town_F.C. "Ipswich Town F.C.") player who made one appearance for the club before his playing career was ended due to injury in a wartime match. Went on to manage [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City_F.C. "Cardiff City F.C.") and [Swansea Town](/wiki/Swansea_City_A.F.C. "Swansea City A.F.C.") and was appointed secretary of the [Football Association of Wales](/wiki/Football_Association_of_Wales "Football Association of Wales") in 1971. Was also later awarded an [OBE](/wiki/Order_of_the_British_Empire "Order of the British Empire") for his services to Welsh football.[[44]](#cite_note-49)
* 5 February 2003: [Dale Roberts](/wiki/Dale_Roberts_(footballer_born_1956) "Dale Roberts (footballer born 1956)"), 46, assistant manager of [Ipswich Town](/wiki/Ipswich_Town_F.C. "Ipswich Town F.C.") from 1995 to 2002, died of cancer. He played 24 league games at centre-half for the [Suffolk](/wiki/Suffolk "Suffolk") club between 1974 and 1980 before signing for [Hull City](/wiki/Hull_City_A.F.C. "Hull City A.F.C."), spending six years at [Boothferry Park](/wiki/Boothferry_Park "Boothferry Park") and helping the club rise from the Fourth Division to the Second, narrowly missing out on a place in the First Division before he retired through injury in 1986 after 182 league games for the Tigers. He was in charge of Hull's youth team from 1989 to 1993 and was also assistant to [George Burley](/wiki/George_Burley "George Burley") at [Ayr United](/wiki/Ayr_United_F.C. "Ayr United F.C.") and [Colchester United](/wiki/Colchester_United_F.C. "Colchester United F.C.") before following him back to [Portman Road](/wiki/Portman_Road "Portman Road"), where he helped the club win promotion to the Premier League in 2000 and finish fifth a year later, although relegation back to Division One occurred after just two seasons.
* 29 May 2003: [Trevor Ford](/wiki/Trevor_Ford "Trevor Ford"), 79, played 401 games in English and Dutch leagues as a forward for [Swansea](/wiki/Swansea_City_A.F.C. "Swansea City A.F.C."), [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C."), [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C."), [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City_F.C. "Cardiff City F.C."), [PSV Eindhoven](/wiki/PSV_Eindhoven "PSV Eindhoven") and [Newport County](/wiki/Newport_County_A.F.C. "Newport County A.F.C.") between 1946 and 1961, scoring 199 goals – peaking at Sunderland where he scored 67 goals in 108 games. He was also one of the leading goalscorers for the [Welsh national side](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team"), scoring 23 times in 38 appearances, but was not included in the squad for the [1958 World Cup](/wiki/1958_FIFA_World_Cup "1958 FIFA World Cup").
* 26 June 2003: [Marc-Vivien Foé](/wiki/Marc-Vivien_Fo%C3%A9 "Marc-Vivien Foé"), 28, Cameroon midfielder who spent the 2002–03 season on loan to [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") from French side Lyon, collapsed and died during a [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") [Confederations Cup](/wiki/Confederations_Cup "Confederations Cup") game. A post mortem revealed that Foé, who had played for West Ham United earlier in his career, had suffered from an undetected heart condition.

## Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002%E2%80%9303_in_English_football&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Events")]

[Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") are fined £5,000 for the state of the pitch at [Stamford Bridge](/wiki/Stamford_Bridge_(stadium) "Stamford Bridge (stadium)") for their [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") match against [Charlton Athletic](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_F.C. "Charlton Athletic F.C.") on 11 January. Chelsea were going to relay their pitch immediately after the match and had removed the old grass surface, leaving only the sand base on which the new pitch would be laid. Chelsea did not inform Charlton of the poor state of the pitch prior to the game, which Chelsea won 4–1.[[45]](#cite_note-50)
